title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will explore kaitiakitanga through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant local waterway that holds deep cultural, spiritual, and historical importance to Ngāti Kahungunu. The stream's name references the native tree (mangakōtukutuku) and its connection to local pūrākau (traditional stories) about environmental balance and community responsibility.
learningObjectives: Identify and explain at least three traditional Māori practices of kaitiakitanga used to care for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Analyze current environmental challenges facing the stream using water quality testing and historical data comparison
Collaboratively develop and present a kaitiakitanga action plan that integrates mātauranga Māori with modern conservation approaches
Articulate personal and collective responsibilities as kaitiaki of local waterways through written reflection and community presentation

activities.title: Whakapapa o Te Awa: Story of Our Stream
activities.description: Students begin with a karakia and waiata for the awa. Local kaumātua shares pūrākau about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, explaining its significance to Ngāti Kahungunu. Students create visual timelines showing the stream's history from pre-colonial times to present day.

activities.title: Mātai Awa: Stream Investigation
activities.description: Field trip to three sites along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students conduct water quality tests (pH, clarity, macroinvertebrate survey) and document human impacts. They compare findings with historical data from regional council records and oral histories.

activities.title: Kōrero with Kaitiaki: Community Perspectives
activities.description: Students interview local kaitiaki including Ngāti Kahungunu representatives, regional council staff, and local farmers. They use structured questions to understand different perspectives on stream management and protection responsibilities.

activities.title: Mahi Tahi: Collaborative Action Planning
activities.description: Working in small groups, students develop kaitiakitanga action plans that blend traditional knowledge with modern science. Plans must address specific issues identified during their investigation and include measurable goals for improvement.

activities.title: Whakanui: Celebration of Learning
activities.description: Students present their kaitiakitanga plans to whānau and community members at the streamside. They perform waiata they've composed about the awa and facilitate discussions about implementing their proposed actions.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua and local kaitiaki as cultural experts
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'State of the Environment' monitoring data for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Water testing kits and macroinvertebrate identification guides from NIWA
'Te Mana o te Wai' framework documents from Ministry for the Environment

tasks: Written report analyzing the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using both scientific data and cultural indicators
Group presentation of kaitiakitanga action plan with specific, measurable objectives
Individual reflection journal documenting personal understanding of kaitiakitanga responsibilities

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L4: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
Social Studies L4: Understand how the ways in which leadership of groups is acquired and exercised have consequences for communities and societies
Social Studies L4: Understand how people's management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability
Health and PE L4: Demonstrate respect for others and develop a sense of social justice
